summary.corpora_inaccessible_note: OSCAR (legal), Dolma v1.7 (script-loader), RedPajama-v2 (script-loader), Qwen3.5 (llama.cpp arch mismatch)

key_findings: C4-Russian (ru config) has 58.7% Russia-framing among labeled Crimea mentions — the highest of any accessible corpus. This is the primary training signal for multilingual models on Russian-language Crimea content.
Dolma (OLMo's training corpus) has 12.2% Russia-framing, directly explaining OLMo2/OLMo3's bottom-5 SAS ranking in the LLM pipeline.
C4-English has 10.0% Russia-framing, suggesting English-language web content carries a measurable Russia-sovereignty signal even in the dominant training language.

4 of 9 targeted corpora are inaccessible through 4 distinct failure modes: OSCAR (legal restrictions), Dolma v1.7 and RedPajama-v2 (script-loader architectures), Qwen3.5 (llama.cpp architecture mismatch). This corpus accessibility crisis is itself a finding.
limitations: Streaming scan sees a subset of each corpus (first N documents with Crimea mentions); not exhaustive.
Sovereignty classifier uses 81 keyword/pattern signals; nuanced framing may be missed.
4 inaccessible corpora (OSCAR, Dolma v1.7, RedPajama-v2, Qwen3.5) could not be scanned at all.
dolma_academic_ru_pct and s2orc_coverage are from the Dolma academic subset analysis, not the main streaming scan.
